items.q: What is the derivative of (3x + 1)^2 with respect to x?
items.correct: 6(3x + 1) — by the chain rule, multiplying by the derivative of the inside (3).
items.misconception: The derivative is 2(3x + 1) — applying the power rule without the chain rule.
items.random_wrong: The derivative is 9x^2 + 6x + 1 by leaving it as it is.
items.why: Students apply the power rule mechanically and forget to multiply by the derivative of the inner function.

items.q: What is the derivative of a constant, like d/dx(5)?
items.correct: 0 — constants do not change with x.
items.misconception: The derivative of 5 is 5 because the constant stays the same.
items.random_wrong: The derivative of 5 is 1 because all constants differentiate to 1.
items.why: Students confuse 'value of a constant' with 'rate of change' — they don't see that 'no change' means derivative zero.

items.q: Does the limit of f(x) as x approaches a have to equal f(a)?
items.correct: No — the limit can exist even if f(a) is undefined or different from the limit.
items.misconception: The limit is always just the value of f at that point — that's how you compute it.
items.random_wrong: Limits always equal zero unless the function is constant.
items.why: Students learn limits by substituting (for continuous functions) and don't realise limits and function values are conceptually distinct.
It's 3y² dy/dx. “3y²” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
The answer examiners want is: (dy/dt)/(dx/dt). “dx/dy” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: dy/dx for x=t, y=t²+1 at t=3
items.why: The real figure is 6. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
